item.title: Upvote for me!
item.content:encoded:
At the start, i'm thinking, " Oh boy. Another killing machine. "
Then boom. Cadburry. Had a good laugh, there.
Then came the downfall. It's just… wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hilariously awesome! I do love the approach of old entities and monsters becoming tempted by the strange new creations of humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Kirby, thanks for the review!

First, you're entirely right. Using bold, italics, subscript etc. for emphasis is a bit of a crutch for me. Ellipses too. In contrast, my latest article feels a bit too "novel like", leaning on italicized exposition. I'm working to strike a balance.

My real issue here was I couldn't find a better way to write 4338 other than all-caps. The tone-shift when it begins speaking in lower-case after having it's spirit broken just worked too well, while saying very little. I think after I threw "official interview writing protocol" out the window with that, all the other unnecessary typographical emphasis sort of crept in. I started writing comic book style.

I'm glad you enjoyed the article. I'll keep working to produce more, and iron out these quirks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read. I wanted to reinforce the unpredictability of this entity, but you're right. Even with all the variables, the Foundation would likely have one set statistic by now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty funny honestly. It has a good juxtaposition between the almighty and the inane, and the idea of a god becoming overweight from poor eating is pretty good.

While I enjoyed this though, I have a few nitpicks about your interview transcripts though:

+ show block
– hide block

One just sort of overall thing I noticed is you use "…" a lot. It's a good tool to demonstrate a pause in the dialogue, but if you use it too much, it begins to stick out to the reader. At least, it started to stick out to me. I think that you could replace this with commas in some places, or maybe lean back on the number of times a character trails off in the middle of a sentence, at least in quick succession.

Greetings, oh 4 3 3 8.

I would imagine that the foundation would either have it say "4338", or "four-three-three-eight". It's not like, bad as is. It just feels out of place in an official transcript.

shocker I mean, your servant

Similar sort of thing here with "shocker". The small formatting feels unbefitting of an in-universe document. It would be less dynamic, but if you just put "herself//" in front of that word, or maybe "her breath//", you could convey the same information.

CAN'T? CAN'T!? YOU

Two things with this one. You already express that this thing is shouting via the caps. The "!?" feels redundant, and somewhat out of place in a transcript (honestly, I would say that you could just note somewhere that this character is always yelling and not use the caps lock too, because that also feels off, but I didn't really notice that as I was reading as much as the other things I pointed out).

Secondly, I think putting the second "can't" in italics instead of bold reads better, because all your lines start with a bold dialogue tag, so it feels like the dialogue tag has reappeared in the middle of the line (at least for me).

ENOUGH

Same comment about the bold in dialogue.

Long post collapsed. ~Zyn

But yeah. I upvoted, because the characters were really engaging and the story was well done, but that doesn't mean that this stuff bothered me. Overall though this is a quality piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
have a 78-83% chance of being accepted by SCP-4338

I think there will.be only one number if it came from statistics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah! Thanks for the read :) You interpreted Thornborrow exactly as I'd wanted to write her. Her final line was intended to be exactly that, the bow at the end of a duel. Not to disparage those who read the ending as a mercy, or sign of respect. This is fiction, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the read! I'm glad you caught on that early, this wasn't intended to be much of a mystery piece.

I'm also really interested in the responses I've been getting. What part of the story resonates with people the most, and what feeling they're left with by the end is really telling of their personality and experiences. There have been a lot of somber responses from people who've struggled with weight loss, and as you said, a lot of surprisingly sympathetic feelings towards 4338.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article has an excellent progression. The schadenfreude I get out of this is incredible. Seeing this monstrous, petty, tyrant depowered, its spirit broken… It's so satisfying. And Thornborrow's little flourish at the end, just *chef's kiss*. Making it get too fat to escape the volcano is just brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured out what was going on as soon as I read that it ballooned to a size of 4.6km3. I instantly thought "Oh man. It got fat."

I'm surprised that the general reaction to SCP-4338's plight here is one of sympathy and pity. I think they got what was coming to them for being so entitled and arrogant. Certainly, Thornborrow may be reveling a bit too much in her cruelty, and the god may simply not have known any better, but the Foundation doesn't really have any reason to have the god's best interests in mind. The god mostly got what they deserved, and their current containment is simply imprisonment for their crimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah! I'm just some goober who likes writing stories. Thanks for the read though, I really appreciate it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly incredible. It’s subtle, and it’s not a standard SCP. It has characters, Character development, and a rollercoaster of emotions going from the terrifying to the silly to the genuinely sad. You, sir, are an inspiration and a genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the read!

For your question: probably not. I try to write self-contained stories more than interconnected stories, save some recurring themes and universe tie-ins. Expect more articles, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this scip.

Really and truly loved it.

Not even the old gods are immune to the plagues of today.

+1.

Are we going to see more Volcano God legends or any other anomolous tales from the islanders in general in the near future?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, don't go dissecting it for a grand opus on self-destructive consumption and entitlement, or the abject emptiness those in positions of extreme power experience when their day is done. There's a sprinkling of that, but honestly, it's just supposed to be story about a volcano god who gets too chonky.

Thanks for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you bit that bullet! I'm trying hard to work my way into the "established author" circle, where users can see my username, and trust that the articles length and complexity is worth their time. I don't know if I'll ever get to, say, TheGreatHippo or djkaktus levels, but I'll do my best!

Thanks for the read, and I'm glad the article hit the notes I was going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m probably reaching, but is there any kind of social commentary to be picked up here? Or is it not that deep and I’m thinking too hard?

Either way, a really funny and interesting read! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit I avoided this one for a while: I'd had Log Fatigue and was worried about committing to the (apparent) length of it. Very glad that I finally bit that bullet: this is fantastic!

It's rare that I feel pity for an SCP, but usually I know to expect it from the beginning. To watch this one diminish over time is at first entertaining, but that final interview log really left a bit of a gut punch…especially since I've seen it happen to friends as well, this carries a surprising amount of significance and sadness.

Excellent job. Incredibly entertaining and it got me thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Starts vaguely scary, gets funny in the middle, ends kinda sad. Well done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always suspected 682 just needs a snickers. Too hangry. 173 probably just necks to SNAP into a slim Jim.

Thanks for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like so many have said, this was hilarious. Maybe the Foundation can try offering up junk food to other Gods. Give the DEER a giant salt lick for starters!

EDIT: Just noticed as of giving you my +1 you are three short of the 200 mark, so congratulations!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers, thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh! Looking at the page history, it seems like this one was put in by Voct? Though previous versions say "pinapple" so I think it was a miss-correct.

So, thank you Voct and thank you MrGoomba!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
30-40kg fruits (e.g., mango, pinrapple, cherimoya, jackfruit).

Pinrapple?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way I see it, even before killing Rowell, this thing has been extorting and killing people for what has likely been millennia. Maybe Thornborrow is enjoying the current situation a bit too much, but 4338 has had it coming for a long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was a twist that I definitely was not expecting. Very well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the read! Here was my intention when it came to how the Foundation refers to 4338:

  • In the primary description, it's evident that this entity has had multiple names throughout it's history interacting with humanity.
  • While it can communicate through proxies, the first interview makes it clear it's fully, and willfully, illiterate.
  • While it's centuries of experience makes it innately aware of group hierarchy, the way it describes the world betrays it's deep ignorance of modern developments.
  • The final interview also details that it was fully convinced the Foundation, and humanity at large, still feared and worshiped it.

Taking all these elements into account, I wanted to imply (especially with the spacing of 4 3 3 8 in the first interview of the second offset) that SCP-4338 believed it's designation was a grandiose, divine title. It may understand numbers, but it could just as well believe the Foundation were a society of number-worshipers. The number "4338" could be holy to them, or represents some important calendar date. It wouldn't care, really. Names are just another human quirk to it, being a singular and self-proclaimed "Godlike" being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic. My only complaint, and I admit that this is kind of a personal pet peeve of mine, but I don't think it makes much sense for Foundation personnel to directly call the entity by its SCP number designation during interviews. This "god" seems like the type that would demand that they refer to it by a suitably grandiose divine title.

Although I could see the director passive aggressively calling it by its SCP number after it asks if she still fears it, just to emphasize how far it has fallen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's clear now for me that Thornborrow was not really being sympathetic, rather, she was just being formal. I do still feel sad about 4338. It's like having a powerful villain that you really hate and want to hurt and that when you do get the chance you just don't feel like it anymore since that villain now is incredibly weak and is in your absolute mercy.

It feels "bad" to kill or hurt him in his weak state because you feel like you are no different from a villain that is taking advantage of weaker beings. It feels unfair even though if the situation is reversed and the villain will not probably feel the same, it still doesn't feel fair. I guess that the difference of being a villain, a hero, or an anti-hero.

Again, I like this. I would also like to add how wonderfully "short" everything is making the task of reading the whole thing not tedious at all. Well, everything was interesting so maybe the length didn't really matter.

Would +1 again if possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cheers :) I'm glad you enjoyed it!

While, of course, what the reader takes away from an article is entirely their own, I really didn't intend Thornborrow to be sympathetic, or heartwarming in any way. She watched a close friend, a mentor senselessly killed by this "God", only to have it parade around in a magma golem duplicate of her. It's killed whole cultures, and hundreds of Foundation personnel. Thornborrow hates SCP-4338, and by the end of the article, she revels in it's suffering, albeit in a composed, civil sort of way. She's a professional, just sarcastic and short-tempered one.

I'm so pleased I was able to evoke sympathy for a megalomaniacal, genocidal monster in so many readers. It was a great exercise in character writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers :) I know this was supposed to be a more humorous article, but at least some of it had to be observational. I've struggled with poor eating habits, and I tried to reflect that a bit here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much ManyManyManyMeats! Sorry I overlooked you on the crit list, I've been trying to get better about noting down all the people I exchange drafts with. I'll add you right now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah, thanks :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! I've been working hard at developing my character writing, and I'm glad this article delivered!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my, this is really good! The first version was the standard scary stuff but amusing. The second version was very amusing, and the third and final one somehow made me sad but was also, for some reason, heartwarming?

+1

Edit: I also wanted to say that one of the things that made it somehow "heartwarming" is because of how Thornborrow still somehow "pity" respected 4338 but seeing your recent reply to a comment and also remembering this line

Use of SCP-4338 for the disposal of slaughterhouse trimmings, industrial runoff, and all other edible waste-products is under consideration.

just made me even sadder.I mean, it's his fault for being a murderous jerky "god" but still, I feel bad for my Vulcan boi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are few articles here that really let you reflect on both your own personality, ambition and willpower as well as society at large, but this does and then some! Lovely!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Greatly improved from draft. Congrats on your success! This is a good article made all the better for the time you've spent refining it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how the god's diet slowly gets more unhealthy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap this was amazing.

You managed to make a perfect transition from intimidating and scary, to hilarious, and finally saddening. You made me feel bad for this lazy, megalomaniacal, volcano couch potato god. Both he, Emerson, and Director Thornborrow are all great and engaging characters.

Keep up the good work. Hard +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I re wrote that "speed and dimensions" line a good dozen times. I wanted to find the perfect blend of clinical and coherent when saying "volcano ball is 2 chonky now".

I'm also glad the characters came off as realistic. I was affraid 4338 would be too over the top, or Thornborrow too snarky, but so far people seem to be pleased with the balance!

What's really surprising is so many people have commented on the sympathetic nature of 4338, but said nothing on the quiet cruelty of Thornborrow. I don't think I illustrated well enough just how much hatred she has for 4338, the thing that killed her friend and mentor. How, by the end of it, she really does revel in the sorry state this "God" has been reduced to. Either way, thanks so much for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a new favorite scp of mine now!
I absolutely love how it builds up this fearsome volcano god, then proceeds to devolve it into what is basically a child throwing a tantrum when they don’t get their sweets. Clicking on the first update and seeing all the healthy, previous options replaced with things like pizzas and junk foods just about killed me dead.
Also this line in particular was great, in a more subtle way, “unlike six months prior, it now lacked the appropriate speed and dimensions.“
Definitely a good plot twist that doesn’t come out of no where, and is very well executed.
The more somber ending was definitely the right way to go. Kept it from being a pure comedy show and made us remember that these are real people (and entity) with real emotions. Somehow made me sympathize with a murderous god? Kudos for somehow pulling that off!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much like "Stalled Conversation", I seem to have found my niche with "Things stuck inside other things who seem like jerks at first, but hit you with the sympathy at the last minute."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to hear :) I was a bit wary of delving into listpages / "revision" articles, but I'm glad it's worked out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be the funniest article I have ever read. Kinda scary at first, but once I caught on, it didn't fail to deliver at any point. I can easily see this becoming one of the top SCPs of the year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh man. at first, i was kinda pissed off by this god but then i feel bad for the god

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This easily could have ended on the first page, but I'm glad it didn't. The article's direction was both unexpected and pleasant, and I enjoyed it thoroughly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good eye! Fixed that. Also, thanks for the read. I'm glad you enjoyed the character :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much! I'm more than willing to make the mystery well a bit more shallow for the sake of a more enjoyable read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really solid. The interviews where he acts like a toddler? Perfect. The way he says AM EEL YA? Perfect.

(just a heads up tho theres a typo in the second version: "you servant" should be your servant)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is magnificent. As soon as I saw the increased size of the sphere in the second iteration, I had suspicions about where this was going, but the execution was far beyond what I expected. You got my vote on the first page, and I regret I couldn't give another for each addition.

item.title: Re: Vulcan, the Disaster
item.content:encoded: FYI, the Pixabay image was uploaded there prior to January 9, 2019; which means it's actually public domain under a CC0 (because, given its upload date, those were the terms it was originally uploaded under.) I've tried to get the Pixabay people to make this a bit clearer on the image pages, but so far they've elected to falsely label the old images under the new license instead for some reason (even though they've recognized on the forum that it's technically inaccurate and the new license only actually applies to the newer uploads).

item.title: Re: Vulcan, the Disaster
item.content:encoded: Corrected! Thanks for spotting that!

item.title: Re: Vulcan, the Disaster
item.content:encoded:
Dope article, it get's a +1 from me!

Just a heads up of something I noticed, in the first offset, Thornborrow misnames the entity '4 2 2 9'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks TheMightyMcB :) As always, I appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best, and only test I care about! Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers! Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Modern Solutions?
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to upvote this at least 3 separate times while reading. Excellent stuff, as per usual.
